output.summary: Review and update CallToAction block for accessibility compliance, bug fixes, and test page modernization. The block is mostly well-implemented but the existing test page lacks DemoContainer wrappers and is missing several required test scenarios. Minor accessibility and PHP issues identified.

accessibilityAudit.currentState: The CallToAction block has good baseline accessibility: it uses a semantic <section> element, the theme_block_props() helper automatically adds aria-labelledby pointing to the heading ID (generated by ThemeHeading via theme_block_region_id()), and buttons use proper <a> elements with accessible labels. The background image uses wp_get_attachment_image which provides alt text from the media library. The dark mode gradient overlay ensures text readability over images.
missingRequirements: Background image in the image variant is purely decorative (behind text overlay) but uses the media library alt text rather than being explicitly marked as decorative with alt='' - this causes screen readers to announce the background image description which is confusing in context
No role='img' with aria-hidden='true' on the background image since it is decorative (overlaid with gradient and text)

The TSX editor component does not mirror the aria-labelledby pattern from PHP (useBlockProps does not add it) - this is acceptable since editor accessibility is handled by WordPress core, but worth noting
recommendations: Mark the background image as decorative by passing alt='' in the wp_get_attachment_image call in CallToAction.php since it serves as a purely decorative background behind text content
Add aria-hidden='true' to the background image element to fully hide it from assistive technology
Ensure the gradient overlay provides sufficient contrast ratio for text over images (currently uses rgba overlays which appear adequate)

fileChanges.description: Mark background image as decorative for accessibility
fileChanges.details: Change wp_get_attachment_image call to pass 'alt' => '' and add 'aria-hidden' => 'true' to the attributes array. The background image is purely decorative (used behind gradient overlays and text content), so screen readers should not announce it. Currently it uses whatever alt text is set in the media library, which causes confusing announcements like 'Demo landscape photo of campus grounds' when the image is just a visual backdrop.

scenarios.demoTitle: No Heading - Description and Buttons Only
scenarios.demoDescription: Tests rendering without a heading. Only description and buttons. The aria-labelledby should not be present since there is no heading to reference. Verifies graceful handling of this edge case.

attributes.description: Sometimes you might want a CTA without a heading, just descriptive text and action buttons.

designDecisions.question: Should the background image in the image variant be marked as decorative (alt='') since it is behind gradient overlays and text content?
designDecisions.recommendation: Yes - mark as decorative with alt='' and aria-hidden='true'. The image serves as a visual backdrop and does not convey meaningful content. Screen readers currently announce the media library alt text which is confusing in this context.

designDecisions.question: Is the current gradient overlay sufficient for WCAG AA contrast on the image background variant?
designDecisions.recommendation: The current gradient uses rgba(31,31,29,0.5) at 85.991% and rgba(31,31,29,0.2) across the full area. Combined with the dark class setting white text, this should provide adequate contrast for most images. However, extremely bright or high-contrast images could still pose readability challenges. The current implementation is acceptable and matches common patterns.
